title: Spotting and treating impacted crop in a hen
subtitle: The hard lump under the chin that the daily glance misses if you do not feel for it.
excerpt: A hen with an impacted crop carries a hard or doughy mass in the pouch at the base of her neck where her food should be moving on overnight. Caught early it clears at home with massage and water; left a fortnight it gets a hen put down. A walk through how to feel for it, how to tell impacted from sour, and the home treatment that works for most cases.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Laying Hens. AHDB Poultry parasite and digestive disorders factsheet. River Cottage Handbook 13 (Chickens), digestive troubles chapter. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive (the crop section is recognisable; modern poultry has moved on but the anatomy chapter still holds).

glossaryTerms.definition: The expandable pouch in a chicken's throat where swallowed food softens before passing into the proventriculus. Sits visibly to the right of the breastbone after a feed and is empty by dawn.
A yeast infection of the crop, often Candida albicans, that turns the crop contents into a smelly liquid. Linked to long-term impaction or to courses of antibiotics.
The chicken's first true stomach, where digestive enzymes begin to break down the food held in the crop. Sits just behind the crop in the gut tract.
Insoluble crushed flint or granite a chicken swallows and holds in the gizzard to grind down food. Free-choice grit is what lets a chicken eat long fibres and whole grain without impaction.

recipeTools.notes: The fix that stops recurrence is free-choice grit, every day.

content.text: The crop is the pouch at the base of a hen's neck where the food she has just eaten sits and softens before passing into the rest of her gut. It fills through the afternoon and empties overnight. By dawn it should be flat and barely findable through the feathers. An impacted crop is one that has stayed full because the contents will not move on.

The hen carrying one looks fine for a few days. Then she stands a little apart, her tail comes down, her appetite drops, and her body condition starts to slip because nothing she eats is reaching her gut. The lump under her chin is the only outward sign. The fix is straightforward in the first week; the chances drop fast after that.
How to feel for a crop
Pick the hen up under one arm so her keel bone runs along your forearm. Find the V where the wishbone meets the breastbone, then move your fingertips up into the loose skin just above. The
crop

sits there, slightly to the right side of the neck. After a good afternoon's feed it should feel like a soft sock full of damp porridge: pliable, warm, and giving under finger pressure. By morning, before she has had anything, it should be flat enough that you struggle to find it at all.
What different crops feel like

attrs.title: Crop condition by feel
attrs.body: Normal full crop: soft, pliable, warm, gives under pressure. Empty morning crop: flat, hard to find. Impacted crop: hard or firm, sometimes the size of a small plum, still present in the morning before the bird has eaten. Sour crop: balloon-soft, slack, full of liquid that you can hear sloshing if you tilt the bird gently. Sour and impacted often happen together; the impaction comes first.

content.text: What causes an impaction

Long fibrous material is the usual cause. A hen given the run of an overgrown patch in spring strips dry grass stalks faster than her gizzard can grind them. Tough kitchen scraps (the stringy ends of leeks, lengths of garden twine pecked off the line) are the same problem. The other route is a shortage of
grit

in the gizzard. Without grit the gizzard cannot grind, the food does not reduce, and the crop above starts to back up. Most well-fed flocks on commercial layer pellets still want a free-choice grit feeder, and the working keeper checks it weekly.
When to treat at home and when to stop

A firm crop caught within the first two or three days, with the bird still bright and eating a little, clears at home in most cases. A doughy crop that has been there a week, with the bird losing condition, is borderline; treatment can still work but the odds are slimmer. A balloon-soft crop full of fluid that smells sour when you tip the bird (mouth slightly open, smell what comes out) has turned into
sour crop
and needs a vet's call, because the yeast infection wants different management. Trying to empty a sour crop by holding the bird upside down at home is the route to inhalation pneumonia.
The home treatment for an impaction

Lift the affected bird into a quarantine pen separate from the flock. Pull all food. Provide a shallow bowl of warm water with a teaspoon of olive oil floated on the surface; many hens with a soft impaction will drink steadily from this and the oil softens the contents. Leave her overnight with water only.

The next morning, with the bird on your lap, work the crop gently between your fingers in a slow downwards-then-circular massage for five minutes. The aim is to break up the hard mass without damaging the crop wall, which is thinner than it feels. Do this for five minutes morning and evening for two days. Many cases clear inside 48 hours: the crop softens, then empties, then starts to fill normally again with the small amounts of soft food you reintroduce.

Reintroduce food slowly. Day one back on food: warm scrambled egg, small amounts, twice. Day two: layer mash made wet to a porridge. Day three: a half ration of layer pellets soaked. Day four: back on full pellets. Resume normal flock life once the morning crop runs flat for three days in a row.
When to call the vet

If the crop has not softened by the third morning of treatment, the impaction is set and the bird needs a vet who can either flush the crop under sedation or perform a small crop incision to lift the mass out. The vet bill rarely matches the value of a backyard hen, and many keepers choose to cull at this point rather than push further; both are acceptable. The decision the hen cannot wait for is the next two days of standing apart with nothing reaching her stomach.
Preventing the next one

Three habits cover most impactions. Keep a grit feeder permanently topped up with mixed insoluble grit, separate from the layer feed, where the birds choose what they need. Strim or scythe long grass before turning hens onto it in spring so the fibres are short enough for the gizzard to grind. Keep garden twine, sisal string, and orange synthetic baler twine off the ground anywhere the flock has access; a length of twine in the crop is the worst-case impaction and the hardest to clear at home.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Crop-trouble outcomes
attrs.intro: What different presentations point to, and what to do next.

items.symptom: Firm crop in the morning, bird still bright.
items.fix: Pull food, offer warm water with olive oil overnight, massage for two days. Most clear inside 48 hours.

items.symptom: Doughy crop, bird losing condition, has been off colour a week.
items.fix: Try the home treatment for 48 hours. If no softening, call the vet for assessment, or take the cull decision.

items.symptom: Balloon-soft fluid crop, smells sour when bird is tipped.
items.cause: Sour crop, yeast overgrowth on top of impaction or after antibiotics.
items.fix: Call the vet. Do not hold the bird upside down at home; the inhalation risk is real.

items.symptom: Crop empties overnight but refills hard the next afternoon.
items.cause: Underlying gizzard or grit issue, or a foreign body in the gut.
items.fix: Top up the grit feeder, switch the bird to soaked feed for a week, watch for a second occurrence. A repeat episode is a vet visit.

items.symptom: Crop trouble in several birds within a fortnight.
items.cause: Something in the flock environment: long-grass turnout, baler twine in reach, mouldy feed.
items.fix: Find the source. Mouldy pellets at the bottom of a feeder is the most common cause in winter; tip the feeder, scrub it out, refill with fresh.

content.text: Note on terminology

Some older poultry books call this a crop-bound hen. The phrase is identical in meaning to impacted crop and you will see both used. The condition behind it (the food sitting in the crop and not passing through to the
proventriculus
) is the same.
